title: Revisiting radio synchrotron diagnostics in star-forming galaxies
comment: 19 pages, 14 figures. Submitted to MNRAS
summary: Radio continuum observations are widely used to study cosmic ray (CR) electron populations and transport processes in star-forming galaxies, but their interpretation relies on several simplifying assumptions. Here, we revisit three common assumptions: that some vertical radio profiles can be explained by CR advection alone, that radio spectra directly trace the galaxy-wide CR electron spectrum, and that bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses are negligible for radio-emitting electrons. We model radio emission using time-dependent CR electron evolution in a magnetohydrodynamical simulation of an isolated Milky Way-mass galaxy. CR electron spectra are evolved self-consistently along Lagrangian tracer particles with the CREST framework, including injection at supernova remnants, advection with the gas, and spatially and temporally varying radiative losses. We compare these results to commonly adopted steady-state models. We find that advection-only transport in self-consistently driven galactic winds fails to reproduce the extended vertical radio intensity profiles observed in edge-on galaxies, despite reproducing the observed steepening of spectral indices with height. This is because slowly accelerating winds keep electrons in strong cooling environments for too long. Matching observed radio haloes with advection alone requires unrealistically high midplane wind velocities, implying that additional transport or re-acceleration processes are required. Although galaxy-integrated CR electron spectra at radio-emitting energies are similar across models, the resulting synchrotron spectra differ systematically because radio emission is biased toward young electrons in dense, strongly magnetised regions. Finally, we show that bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses significantly shape radio spectra even when their loss rate is subdominant and therefore cannot be neglected.

AI.tldr: This paper uses magnetohydrodynamical simulations with the CREST framework to test three common assumptions about radio continuum emission in star-forming galaxies. It finds that (1) advection-only transport cannot reproduce observed vertical radio profiles, (2) galaxy-integrated CR electron spectra differ from synchrotron spectra due to observational biases, and (3) bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses significantly shape radio spectra even when subdominant.
AI.motivation: To revisit three simplifying assumptions commonly used in interpreting radio continuum observations of star-forming galaxies: that vertical radio profiles can be explained by CR advection alone, that radio spectra directly trace the galaxy-wide CR electron spectrum, and that bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses are negligible for radio-emitting electrons.
AI.method: The authors model radio emission using time-dependent CR electron evolution in a magnetohydrodynamical simulation of an isolated Milky Way-mass galaxy. CR electron spectra are evolved self-consistently along Lagrangian tracer particles with the CREST framework, including injection at supernova remnants, advection with the gas, and spatially and temporally varying radiative losses. Results are compared to commonly adopted steady-state models.
AI.result: (1) Advection-only transport fails to reproduce extended vertical radio intensity profiles despite reproducing spectral index steepening with height, because slowly accelerating winds keep electrons in strong cooling environments too long. (2) Galaxy-integrated CR electron spectra at radio-emitting energies are similar across models, but synchrotron spectra differ because radio emission is biased toward young electrons in dense, strongly magnetised regions. (3) Bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses significantly shape radio spectra even when their loss rate is subdominant.
AI.conclusion: Matching observed radio haloes with advection alone requires unrealistically high midplane wind velocities, implying additional transport or re-acceleration processes are needed. Bremsstrahlung and Coulomb losses cannot be neglected in modeling radio spectra.

title: Characterizing the GD-1 Stream with DESI DR2 Data: Thin Stream and Hot Cocoon
comment: 29 pages, 15 figures, 8 tables, submitted to ApJ, comments are welcome, associated data for all Tables is available on zenodo (https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.19258456) including a jupyter notebook tutorial, see paper for more details
summary: GD-1 is among the longest, coldest stellar streams in the Milky Way, making it an ideal target for probing dark matter substructure through dynamical heating. We present a catalog of 608 spectroscopically confirmed GD-1 members from the first three years of Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) observations. This constitutes the largest homogeneous spectroscopic sample of GD-1, doubling the number of members previously available only through heterogeneous compilations combining multiple surveys with different systematics. Using these data, we derive updated stream tracks in sky position, proper motion, and radial velocity that extend over $100^\circ$ of the stream. We apply a Gaussian mixture model to decompose the stream into a dynamically cold thin component ($σ_V = 2.49\pm 0.28$ km s$^{-1}$, width $= 0.23\pm0.01^\circ$) and a kinematically hot cocoon ($σ_V = 6.13\pm0.75$ km s$^{-1}$, width $= 2.18\pm0.17^\circ$). The cocoon contains $\sim30\%$ of members and its velocity dispersion is consistent with $\sim11$ Gyr of heating by cold dark matter subhalos. We also detect a large proper motion dispersion ($41.36\pm4.98$ km s$^{-1}$) along the stream direction in the cocoon component. This feature indicates a significant line-of-sight distance spread in the cocoon, and its origin will be further explored in a forthcoming paper. These measurements demonstrate the power of DESI spectroscopy for characterizing the multi-component phase-space structure of stellar streams and constraining small-scale dark matter substructure.

AI.tldr: Using 608 spectroscopically confirmed GD-1 stream members from DESI (doubling previous samples), we decompose the stream into a cold thin component (σ_V=2.49 km/s, width=0.23°) and a hot cocoon (σ_V=6.13 km/s, width=2.18°, ~30% of members), with velocity dispersions consistent with ~11 Gyr of heating by cold dark matter subhalos. We also detect large proper motion dispersion in the cocoon indicating significant line-of-sight distance spread.
AI.motivation: GD-1 is one of the longest, coldest stellar streams in the Milky Way, making it an ideal target for probing dark matter substructure through dynamical heating.
AI.method: We present a catalog of 608 spectroscopically confirmed GD-1 members from the first three years of DESI observations, the largest homogeneous spectroscopic sample (doubling previous heterogeneous compilations). We derive updated stream tracks over 100° and apply a Gaussian mixture model to decompose the stream into thin and cocoon components.
AI.result: The cold thin component has σ_V=2.49±0.28 km/s and width=0.23±0.01°. The hot cocoon has σ_V=6.13±0.75 km/s, width=2.18±0.17°, contains ~30% of members, and its velocity dispersion is consistent with ~11 Gyr of heating by cold dark matter subhalos. Large proper motion dispersion (41.36±4.98 km/s) along the stream direction in the cocoon indicates significant line-of-sight distance spread.
AI.conclusion: These measurements demonstrate the power of DESI spectroscopy for characterizing multi-component phase-space structure of stellar streams and constraining small-scale dark matter substructure. The origin of the proper motion dispersion feature will be explored in a forthcoming paper.

title: Light, heavy, primordial: exploring the diversity of black hole seeding and growth mechanisms in the JWST era
comment: Submitted to OJA; comments welcome
summary: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) has revealed a puzzling population of massive black holes in the first billion years, many of which are over-massive compared to their hosts (obese black holes), and reside in metal-poor hosts, posing a challenge for theoretical models at these early epochs. In this work, we compare the observational properties of astrophysically-seeded black holes using the DELPHI semi-analytic model and cosmologically-seeded primordial black holes (PBHs) using the PHANES analytic model. We explore the growth of light ($\sim 100 M_\odot$) and heavy ($\sim 10^{3-5}M_\odot$) seeds through mergers and accretion (both Eddington-limited and at super-Eddington rates) in the astrophysical scenario; PBHs (seeded between $10^{0.5-6}M_\odot$) only grow through accretion at sub-Eddington rates. Comparing to observables at $z \sim 5-10$, the only model that can be ruled out is the one where we allow Eddington-limited accretion onto light seeds. The observed high values of the black hole mass-stellar mass relation ($0.3-1$) can be reproduced by both PBHs and heavy seeds accreting at super-Eddington rates. However, only the PBH and Eddington-limited heavy seeding models can simultaneously reproduce the observed black hole masses (${\rm M_{bh}}$), stellar masses ($M_*$), and extremely low host metallicities ($Z \leq 0.01 Z_\odot$) inferred at $z \sim 7-10$. Crucially, we find PBHs show decrease in the black hole mass-stellar mass ratio with increasing halo mass at all redshifts, contrary to any astrophysical black hole model. Selecting systems at $z \sim 7$ with ${\rm M_{bh}}/M_* > 0.1$ and bolometric luminosities $\sim 10^{44-46} {\rm erg~s^{-1}}$ that show a negative black hole to stellar mass ratio and reside in $10^{9-11}M_\odot$ halos offer a promising clustering-based discriminant of PBH seeding models.

AI.tldr: This paper compares astrophysical black hole seeding (light/heavy seeds) vs. primordial black holes (PBHs) to explain JWST's observed over-massive black holes in metal-poor hosts at z~5-10. Only PBHs and heavy super-Eddington seeds can match the high black hole-to-stellar mass ratios, but PBHs uniquely predict a decreasing Mbh/M* ratio with increasing halo mass, offering a clustering-based discriminant.
AI.motivation: JWST has discovered massive black holes in the early Universe (first billion years) that are over-massive relative to their host galaxies (obese black holes) and reside in metal-poor environments. These observations challenge existing theoretical models of black hole formation and growth at high redshift.
AI.method: The authors compare two seeding scenarios using semi-analytic/analytic models: (1) astrophysical seeds (light ~100 M⊙ and heavy ~10^3-5 M⊙) via DELPHI, growing through mergers and accretion (Eddington-limited or super-Eddington); (2) primordial black holes (PBHs, 10^0.5-6 M⊙) via PHANES, growing only via sub-Eddington accretion. Models are compared to JWST observables at z~5-10.
AI.result: Eddington-limited accretion onto light seeds is ruled out. Both PBHs and heavy seeds accreting at super-Eddington rates reproduce the high Mbh/M* ratios (0.3-1). Only PBH and Eddington-limited heavy seeding models simultaneously match Mbh, M*, and low metallicities (Z ≤ 0.01 Z⊙) at z~7-10. PBHs uniquely show decreasing Mbh/M* with increasing halo mass, opposite to all astrophysical models.
AI.conclusion: Selecting z~7 systems with Mbh/M* > 0.1, bolometric luminosities ~10^44-46 erg/s, negative Mbh/M*-halo mass correlation, and host halo masses 10^9-11 M⊙ provides a promising clustering-based discriminant to identify PBH seeding.

title: Predicting the thermodynamics in the chromosphere from the translation of SDO data into the IRIS$^{2}$ inversion results using a visual transformer model
comment: 21 pages, 6 figures, and 7 tables
summary: We present SDO2IRIS$^2$: a visual transformer model that translates a combination of images of the chromosphere and transition region (TR), observed by AIA, and a line-of-sight magnetogram, provided by HMI, into temperature, line-of-sight velocity (v$_{los}$), velocity of the turbulent motions (v$_{turb}$), and electron density (n$_{e}$) in the chromosphere. Using the thermodynamic variables obtained from the inversion of the chromospheric lines Mg II h&k, observed by IRIS, as the target of the model, and the intensity images in the chromosphere and TR, and the photospheric magnetogram as the input, the predicted T and n$_{e}$ show a strong correlation ($\approx 0.80$) for $\approx$80% of the test inverted data, a moderate-to-strong correlation ($\approx0.63$) for 70% of the v$_{turb}$ of the target test inverted data, while for the $v_{los}$, the correlation is weak. Therefore, the predicted values by SDO2IRIS$^2$ may be used as an estimation of the thermodynamics in the chromosphere, either as a stand-alone result or as complementary information to other chromospheric data observed simultaneously. The execution time employed by SDO2IRIS$^2$ to obtain the thermodynamic values in the chromosphere is of the order of a few minutes, being $\le10$ minutes when using a CPU, and $\le5$ minutes when using a GPU. SDO2IRIS$^2$ opens a new avenue for the use of SDO data thanks to the inversions provided by IRIS observables.

AI.tldr: SDO2IRIS$^2$ is a visual transformer model that translates SDO/AIA and HMI images into chromospheric thermodynamic variables (temperature, line-of-sight velocity, turbulent velocity, electron density) trained on IRIS Mg II h&k inversions, achieving strong correlations for temperature and density, moderate for turbulent velocity, and weak for line-of-sight velocity, with fast execution (≤10 min CPU, ≤5 min GPU).
AI.motivation: To enable estimation of chromospheric thermodynamic variables from widely available SDO data (AIA + HMI) by leveraging inversion results from high-resolution IRIS Mg II h&k spectra, bypassing the need for direct spectroscopic inversions.
AI.method: A visual transformer model trained on AIA chromospheric/TR intensity images and HMI line-of-sight magnetograms as inputs, with target labels derived from thermodynamic inversions of IRIS Mg II h&k lines.
AI.result: Strong correlation (~0.80) for temperature and electron density across ~80% of test data; moderate-to-strong correlation (~0.63) for turbulent velocity across ~70% of test data; weak correlation for line-of-sight velocity. Execution time ≤10 min on CPU, ≤5 min on GPU.
AI.conclusion: SDO2IRIS$^2$ provides a fast, viable method for estimating chromospheric thermodynamics from SDO data, usable standalone or as complementary data, opening new applications for SDO via IRIS-based inversions.

title: Chaotic migration of LISA Extreme Mass Ratio Inspirals in a turbulent accretion disk: effect on waveform de-phasing
comment: 12 pages, 3 figures. Submitted to ApJL. Comments welcome
summary: Gravitational wave (GW) detector LISA will observe near-coalescence extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs), which typically form in galactic central accretion disks. Gas torques on EMRI will alter its GW-driven inspiral trajectory from the vacuum expectation, leading to potentially LISA-observable GW dephasing ($Δψ_{\rm gas}$). Most studies compute $Δψ_{\rm gas}$ for a thin, laminar disk, with negligible flow turbulence, where the disk exerts a fairly well-understood linear torque ($T_{\rm lin}$). However, these disks must be turbulent due to magneto-rotational instability in the inner regions. Hence, we present a proof-of-concept general, agnostic prescription for the turbulent torque ($T_{\rm turb}$) acting on an EMRI by modeling it as a Gaussian distribution around $T_{\rm lin}$, based on recent advances from a global hydrodynamical (HD) study. We compute $Δψ_{\rm gas}$ for the ``golden'' circular EMRI with total source mass $M=10^6~{\rm M}_\odot$ and mass ratio $q=5\times10^{-5}$ in its final four-year evolution at redshift $z=0.276$ and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) $=50$ by varying Eddington ratio ${\rm f}_{\rm Edd}$, turbulence normalization $C$ ($=~360$ in the aforementioned HD study), disk aspect ratio $h_0$, and turbo-viscous coefficient $α$ in a reasonable parameters space. We find that for ${\rm f}_{\rm Edd}\gtrsim0.3$, $C\gtrsim300$, $h_0\gtrsim0.03$, and $α\gtrsim0.1$, gas-induced dephasings are unobservable if only considering $T_{\rm lin}$ but could become detectable ($Δψ_{\rm gas}>8/$SNR) if EMRIs exhibit chaotic migration due to turbulent gas flow. Hence, this work motivates running MHD simulations of accretion disks with embedded LISA EMRIs in the early in-spiral phase over long enough timescales to understand the evolution of their orbital elements and the imprint of the turbulent environment on their gravitational waveforms.

AI.tldr: This paper shows that while gas-induced GW dephasing from linear torques in laminar disks may be unobservable for LISA EMRIs, turbulent torques (modeled as Gaussian distributions) can produce detectable dephasing under certain parameter conditions, motivating future MHD simulations.
AI.motivation: Previous studies computed GW dephasing from gas torques on EMRIs assuming thin, laminar disks with linear torque prescriptions, but real accretion disks in galactic centers are turbulent due to magneto-rotational instability, so the impact of turbulence on EMRI GW signals needs to be assessed.
AI.method: The authors develop a proof-of-concept prescription for turbulent torque modeled as a Gaussian distribution around the linear torque, based on a global hydrodynamical study. They compute the gas-induced GW dephasing Δψ_gas for a "golden" circular EMRI (M=10^6 M⊙, q=5×10^-5, z=0.276, SNR=50) over four years, varying Eddington ratio f_Edd, turbulence normalization C, disk aspect ratio h0, and turbo-viscous coefficient α.
AI.result: For f_Edd≳0.3, C≳300, h0≳0.03, and α≳0.1, gas-induced dephasings are unobservable under linear torque alone but become detectable (Δψ_gas > 8/SNR) when including turbulent chaotic migration.
AI.conclusion: Turbulent torques can make EMRI GW dephasing detectable by LISA where linear torques alone would be insufficient, motivating long-duration MHD simulations of accretion disks with embedded EMRIs to better characterize orbital evolution and turbulent imprints on waveforms.

title: Filter Design for Estimating the Stellar Metallicity of Metal-poor Stars from Gaia XP Spectra
comment: Accepted for publication in The Astronomical Journal. 17 pages, 15 figures
summary: The estimation of stellar atmospheric parameters for large-scale samples, particularly metal-poor stars, is a cornerstone of Galactic archaeology. In this work, we optimized a photometric filter design tailored to measuring stellar metallicities for very metal-poor stars with [Fe/H]$< -1$.The optimal configurations consist of a central wavelength $λ_{\rm c}$ = 3960 Angstrom with a bandwidth $Δλ$ = 80 Angstrom for giant stars, and $λ_{\rm c} $= 3920 Angstrom with $Δλ$ = 80 Angstrom for dwarf stars. By applying these optimized filters to synthetic photometry derived from Gaia XP spectra, we inferred metallicities for both populations. Both internal and external validations demonstrate high precision across a wide metallicity range: 0.18-0.19 dex for $-2 \le \rm [Fe/H] \le -1$, 0.23-0.33 dex for $-3 \le \rm [Fe/H] \le -2$, and approximately 0.39 dex for the most metal-poor regime, successfully extending down to $\rm [Fe/H] \approx -4$ for giant stars, $\rm [Fe/H] \approx -3.3$ for dwarf stars. Finally, we present a catalog of approximately 14.5 million metal-poor stars with robust $\rm [Fe/H]$ measurements, along with more than ten thousand red giant ultra metal-poor candidates with $\rm [Fe/H] < -4.0$, providing a valuable resource for exploring the early formation and chemical evolution of the Milky Way.

AI.tldr: The authors optimize photometric filter designs (3960/80 Å for giants, 3920/80 Å for dwarfs) to measure metallicities of very metal-poor stars ([Fe/H] < -1). Using synthetic photometry from Gaia XP spectra, they achieve high precision down to [Fe/H] ≈ -4 (giants) and -3.3 (dwarfs), producing a catalog of ~14.5 million metal-poor stars and >10,000 ultra metal-poor candidates.
AI.motivation: Accurate stellar metallicity estimation for large-scale samples, especially very metal-poor stars, is essential for Galactic archaeology. Existing photometric systems lack tailored designs for the low-metallicity regime, limiting precision and sample size.
AI.method: Optimized narrowband photometric filter designs centered at 3960 Å (giants) and 3920 Å (dwarfs) with 80 Å bandwidths; synthetic photometry derived from Gaia XP spectra used to infer [Fe/H] for both populations.
AI.result: Precision of 0.18–0.19 dex for [Fe/H] ∈ [-2,-1], 0.23–0.33 dex for [-3,-2], ~0.39 dex for most metal-poor regime; catalog of ~14.5 million metal-poor stars and >10,000 ultra metal-poor candidates with [Fe/H] < -4.
AI.conclusion: The optimized filter design and resulting catalog provide a valuable resource for studying the early formation and chemical evolution of the Milky Way, particularly for the most metal-poor stellar populations.

title: A New Pathway to Single Be Stars: Ejected Companions from Type Ia Supernovae
comment: accepted for publication in RAA
summary: Be stars are rapid rotators generally produced by binary interactions. The single Be stars in the observations pose challenges to the Be star formation theory. In this paper, we propose a new pathway for the formation of single Be stars, in which the Be star is taken as the ejected companion star from a Type Ia supernova (SN Ia) explosion. Recent numerical simulations suggest that explosive oxygen burning, initiated via the convective Urca process in certain helium (He) stars near the Chandrasekhar mass limit, can set off a SN Ia. Based on this proposition, we further demonstrate that about $0.4\%$ of He star + main-sequence (MS) star binaries may evolve into single Be stars, where the MS star is spun up due to the mass accretion from the He star, and then the He star explodes as a SN Ia. We employ detailed binary evolutionary simulations and find the parameter space that would produce single Be stars via the SN Ia channel. Around $22\%$ of Be stars from the SN Ia progenitor channel exhibit peculiar tangential velocities exceeding $24\ \rm km/s$, classifying them as runaway stars. This suggests that the SN Ia channel plays a meaningful role in forming single Be stars, particularly within the runaway star population.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes that single Be stars can form when a main-sequence star accretes mass from a helium star companion, which then explodes as a Type Ia supernova, spinning up the MS star; about 0.4% of He star + MS binaries may evolve this way, and ~22% of such Be stars are runaways with tangential velocities >24 km/s.
AI.motivation: Observations of single Be stars challenge existing binary-interaction-based formation theories; this work aims to explain their origin via a new SN Ia channel.
AI.method: The authors use detailed binary evolutionary simulations based on the premise that explosive oxygen burning via the convective Urca process in He stars near the Chandrasekhar limit triggers a SN Ia, ejecting the companion (now spun up by accretion) as a single Be star.
AI.result: About 0.4% of He star + MS binaries may become single Be stars through this channel; ~22% of such Be stars have runaway tangential velocities >24 km/s.
AI.conclusion: The SN Ia channel contributes meaningfully to the formation of single Be stars, especially among the runaway star population.

title: Constraining Dark Matter Density Profiles in UFDs with Wide Binaries: Forecast for the Chinese Space Station Survey Telescope
comment: 13 pages, 8 figures, 1 table, accepted for publication on MNRAS
summary: The internal structure of dark matter halos on sub-galactic scales remains a key open question, particularly in the context of the core-cusp problem. Ultra-faint dwarf galaxies (UFDs), owing to their extreme dark matter dominance, provide a promising laboratory to probe these density profiles through stellar tracers. In this work, we assess the capability of the Chinese Space Station Telescope (CSST) to detect and characterize wide binary stars in the nearby UFD Segue 1, using mock observations. We generate mock binary populations based on our existing $N$-body simulations and incorporate realistic CSST observational conditions, including the expected deep-field limiting magnitude ($g \sim 27.5$ mag) and a photometric completeness of approximately $90\%$. The two-point correlation function (2PCF) of stellar pairs is used as a statistical tool to recover the binary fraction under these assumptions. We find that CSST can robustly detect wide binaries at the $3σ$ level for binary fractions as low as $f_b \gtrsim 0.01$, provided a stellar sample size of $N_{\mathrm{star}} \gtrsim 2300$. However, distinguishing between cusped and cored dark matter profiles is significantly more demanding, requiring $N_{\mathrm{star}} \gtrsim 6000$ and $f_b \gtrsim 0.1$ within $\sim 40\mathrm{kpc}$.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates the Chinese Space Station Telescope's (CSST) ability to detect wide binary stars in the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Segue 1 and differentiate between cusped and cored dark matter halo profiles using mock observations and two-point correlation functions. CSST can detect binaries at 3σ for fractions ≥0.01 with ≥2300 stars, but distinguishing dark matter profiles requires ≥6000 stars and binary fraction ≥0.1 within ~40 kpc.
AI.motivation: The internal structure of dark matter halos on sub-galactic scales, specifically the core-cusp problem, remains unresolved. Ultra-faint dwarf galaxies (UFDs) offer a promising probe due to their extreme dark matter dominance, and wide binary stars can serve as tracers of the gravitational potential.
AI.method: Mock binary populations were generated from N-body simulations, incorporating realistic CSST observational conditions (limiting magnitude g~27.5 mag, ~90% photometric completeness). The two-point correlation function (2PCF) of stellar pairs was used to statistically recover binary fractions and distinguish dark matter profiles.
AI.result: CSST can detect wide binaries at 3σ for binary fractions f_b ≥ 0.01 with N_star ≥ 2300. Distinguishing cusped vs. cored profiles requires N_star ≥ 6000 and f_b ≥ 0.1 within ~40 kpc.
AI.conclusion: CSST is capable of detecting wide binaries in UFDs, but differentiating dark matter density profiles requires significantly larger stellar samples and higher binary fractions.

title: Investigation of White-light Emission in Compact Flares
comment: accepted by ApJ
summary: White-light flares (WLFs) are usually tend to be those very large flares. Nevertheless, several small and compact WLFs have been reported and thought to be produced by low-height magnetic reconnection. However, whether low-height magnetic reconnection can efficiently produce WLFs remains unclear. For the first time, we conduct a statistical study of the WL emission in compact flares to address this question. Using over a decade observations from the \textit{Solar Dynamics Observatory} (SDO), we identify 28 compact flares, including 19 C-class and 9 B-class flares. We find these compact flares can be classified into three types based on the magnetic configuration of the flare, corresponding to the U-shape loop (type I), the flux emergence near sunspot (type II), and the fan-spine like structure (type III). For each type, the flares numbers are 9 (7 C-calss and 2 B-class), 9 (3 C-calss and 6 B-calss) and 10 (9 C-calss and 1 B-calss), respectively. We find the occurrence rate of WLFs in compact flares is $\sim60.7\%$ (17/28), and for the C-class the rate can be up to $\sim89.5\%$ (17/19). No WLF was found in B-class compact flares. The occurrence rates for three types are $\sim77.8\%$ (7/9), $\sim11.1\%$ (1/9) and 90\% (9/10), respectively. And for the C-class flares, the occurrence rates for three types are 100\% (7/7), $\sim33.3\%$ (1/3) and 100\% (9/9), respectively. Our results suggest type-I and type-III compact flares are more likely to produce WL emissions.

AI.tldr: Statistical study of 28 compact solar flares (19 C-class, 9 B-class) using SDO data shows ~60.7% produce white-light emission. C-class compact flares have ~89.5% WL occurrence. Three magnetic-configuration types are identified: type I (U-shape loop, 77.8% WL), type II (flux emergence near sunspot, 11.1% WL), type III (fan-spine structure, 90% WL). Types I and III are most efficient at producing WL emissions, especially for C-class flares (100% each). No WL emission found in B-class flares.
AI.motivation: To address whether low-height magnetic reconnection can efficiently produce white-light emissions in compact flares, as previous reports were limited to individual events and lacked statistical validation.
AI.method: Statistical analysis of 28 compact flares identified from over a decade of SDO observations. Flares were classified into three types based on magnetic configuration (U-shape loop, flux emergence near sunspot, fan-spine structure). WL emission occurrence rates were computed for each type and flare class (B-class, C-class).
AI.result: Overall WL occurrence rate in compact flares is ~60.7% (17/28); for C-class alone ~89.5% (17/19). Type I: 77.8% WL (100% for C-class). Type II: 11.1% WL (33.3% for C-class). Type III: 90% WL (100% for C-class). No B-class compact flares produced WL emission.
AI.conclusion: Low-height magnetic reconnection can efficiently produce white-light emissions, particularly in type-I (U-shape loop) and type-III (fan-spine) compact flares, where C-class flares show 100% WL occurrence. Type II (flux emergence near sunspot) is inefficient for WL production.

title: Orbital evolution of highly eccentric bodies embedded in a ringed accretion disc
comment: 16 pages, 19 figures. Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: Various processes can induce long-lived overdense rings and arcs in protoplanetary and AGN accretion discs, such as the accumulation of gas at the outer edge of the dead zone, or the infall of material. Using the local approximation of dynamical friction, we investigate the orbital evolution of a low-mass highly-eccentric point-mass accretor (perturber) embedded in an isothermal disc hosting a density ring. We specifically consider the regime in which the eccentricity exceeds four times the disc aspect ratio. For prograde perturbers, orbits that cross the ring progressively circularize while their semi-major axes converge toward the ring radius. As a result, perturbers accumulate, forming a population ring superimposed on the gaseous ring. The ring therefore acts as a migration trap for these eccentric orbits. We also find that prograde orbits tangent to the ring, either at apocentre or pericentre, remain tangential throughout their evolution; perturbers confined to these trajectories experience the highest accretion rates. In contrast, retrograde perturbers always migrate inward. Once the semi-major axis becomes smaller than the ring radius, the eccentricity grows, but not enough for the orbit to intersect the ring again. We also discuss how feedback effects, such as jet launching and thermal torques, could modify the effective forces acting on the perturbers.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the orbital evolution of low-mass eccentric perturbers in accretion discs with density rings, finding that prograde perturbers circularize and accumulate at the ring radius (migration trap), while retrograde perturbers migrate inward.
AI.motivation: To understand how long-lived overdense rings and arcs in protoplanetary and AGN accretion discs affect the orbital evolution of embedded eccentric perturbers, and whether such rings can act as migration traps.
AI.method: Local approximation of dynamical friction to investigate orbital evolution of low-mass highly-eccentric point-mass accretors in an isothermal disc with a density ring, considering eccentricity > 4× disc aspect ratio.
AI.result: Prograde perturbers circularize and converge toward the ring radius, forming a population ring; retrograde perturbers always migrate inward; tangential orbits (apocentre or pericentre) remain tangential and experience highest accretion rates.
AI.conclusion: Density rings in accretion discs act as migration traps for eccentric prograde perturbers, leading to accumulation, while retrograde perturbers do not get trapped. Feedback effects (jets, thermal torques) could modify these dynamics.

title: BV photometry of the ultracompact binary star GP Com
comment: published in C. R. Acad. Bulg. Sci., 79, 285-293 (2026)
summary:
We present optical B and V band photometry of GP Com - an ultracompact binary consisting of an accreting white dwarf and helium secondary component. Our data set contains 7.7 hours observations in V band with the 2.0m telescope and 2.9 hours simultaneous observations in B and V bands with the 1.5m telescope of the Rozhen National Astronomical Obsevatory, Bulgaria. The observations cover of about 13 orbital periods.
  We find an orbital modulation with amplitude 0.04-0.05 mag in B and V bands. Adopting that it is due to a bright spot, we estimate its temperature 19700 \pm 3000 K. We estimate mass accretion rate onto the white dwarf of about 2.10^{-12} M_sun/yr, consistent with the predicted rate for a cool donor.
  The data are available on Zenodo: zenodo.org/records/18768211.

AI.tldr: Optical B/V photometry of GP Com (ultracompact accreting white dwarf + He secondary) from Rozhen Observatory yields orbital modulation (0.04-0.05 mag), bright spot temperature ~19700±3000 K, and mass accretion rate ~2×10⁻¹² M☉/yr consistent with a cool donor.
AI.motivation: To characterize the orbital modulation and physical properties (bright spot temperature, mass accretion rate) of the ultracompact binary GP Com via multi-band photometry.
AI.method: 7.7 hr V-band (2.0m telescope) + 2.9 hr simultaneous B/V-band (1.5m telescope) observations at Rozhen National Astronomical Observatory, covering ~13 orbital periods; photometric analysis of orbital modulation.
AI.result: Orbital modulation amplitude 0.04–0.05 mag in B and V; bright spot temperature 19700±3000 K; mass accretion rate ~2×10⁻¹² M☉/yr.
AI.conclusion: The measured mass accretion rate is consistent with predictions for a cool donor in this ultracompact binary system.

title: Early metal-enriched baryon cycling before the midpoint of cosmic reionization
comment: To be resubmitted after revision; Nature Astronomy
summary: Models predict that chemical enrichment and gas redistribution should proceed rapidly once star formation begins, yet direct observational constraints at the earliest cosmic epochs have been scarce. Here we present evidence that metal-enriched gas in multiple ionic phases was already present around galaxies before the midpoint of cosmic reionization. Using JWST/NIRSpec rest-frame ultraviolet spectroscopy of three galaxies at redshifts $z=7.2-9.3$, we detect blueshifted metal absorption in all three systems; across the sample, the detected transitions span neutral, low-ionization, and high-ionization species, including O I, Si II, C II, Si IV, and C IV. These absorption features show velocity offsets of order $|Δv| \sim 50$--$250\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$, predominantly blueshifted relative to the systemic redshifts of the host galaxies derived from nebular emission lines. This ionic coexistence within a broadly shared velocity structure, together with the observed equivalent-width ratios, is consistent with outflowing or otherwise kinematically disturbed galaxy-associated gas, similar to that seen at lower redshift. The observations therefore indicate that metal-enriched gas associated with galaxies was already kinematically disturbed at very early times, requiring rapid metal production in the early generations of stars. These results show that key conditions for baryon cycling were established in at least a subset of luminous galaxies within the first several hundred million years of cosmic time, well before the completion of reionization.

AI.tldr: JWST/NIRSpec observations of three galaxies at z=7.2–9.3 reveal blueshifted metal absorption in neutral, low-, and high-ionization species (O I, Si II, C II, Si IV, C IV), indicating that metal-enriched, kinematically disturbed gas was already present around galaxies before the midpoint of cosmic reionization, requiring rapid metal production in early stellar generations.
AI.motivation: To observationally constrain when chemical enrichment and gas redistribution began around galaxies in the early Universe, as models predict rapid enrichment after star formation begins but direct evidence at the earliest cosmic epochs has been lacking.
AI.method: JWST/NIRSpec rest-frame ultraviolet spectroscopy of three galaxies at z=7.2–9.3, detecting blueshifted metal absorption across multiple ionic phases (neutral, low-ionization, high-ionization species) and analyzing velocity offsets and equivalent-width ratios.
AI.result: All three galaxies show blueshifted metal absorption (O I, Si II, C II, Si IV, C IV) with velocity offsets |Δv| ~ 50–250 km/s. The ionic coexistence within a shared velocity structure and equivalent-width ratios are consistent with outflowing or kinematically disturbed galaxy-associated gas, similar to lower-redshift analogs.
AI.conclusion: Metal-enriched gas associated with galaxies was already kinematically disturbed before the midpoint of reionization, indicating rapid metal production in early stars and establishing key conditions for baryon cycling within the first several hundred million years of cosmic time.

title: Supermassive Black Hole Winds in X-rays: SUBWAYS IV. Tracing Radio Emission and Unveiling the Role of Winds
comment: 25 pages, to appear in A&A
summary: Most Active Galactic Nuclei (AGN) are Radio Quiet, with radio emission that may arise from star-formation activity, AGN-driven winds, weak jets, and coronal activity. Disentangling these mechanisms is challenging and requires detailed multi-wavelength investigation, but it is crucial for quantifying AGN feedback in galaxy evolution. We present a detailed radio investigation of 21 X-ray selected AGN in the Supermassive Black Hole Winds in X-Rays (SUBWAYS) sample (log Lbol = 44.9-46.3 erg/s, z=0.1-0.5), selected to systematically search for Ultra-Fast Outflows (UFOs). UFOs are detected in 30% of the targets, making the sample particularly well-suited for investigating the role and signatures of multi-scale outflows at different frequencies. We build the radio SED of the sources complementing our proprietary data, collected with the JVLA at 1.5 and 6 GHz, with images from LoTSS and other publicly available radio surveys between 150 and 1400 MHz. We investigate the role and occurrence of the aforementioned mechanisms, with particular interest in outflows and their possible relation with UFOs. We combined information on spectral indices, luminosities, and morphologies of the radio emission with properties derived in other wavebands, such as Star Formation Rate, X-ray luminosity, Eddington ratio or the UFO kinetic luminosity. All the sources are detected and are mostly consistent with RQ AGN. For 80% of the sources the data suggest the presence of an outflow (wind or weak jet). Interestingly, our results indicate that AGN with UFOs tend to have larger radio extension and a steep radio spectrum consistent with outflows. Moreover, the radio emission of the 6 UFO hosts is consistent with predictions from wind-driven shock models, possibly indicating a direct connection between the two phases. Alternatively, this may reflect physical conditions favouring the rise of both phenomena.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes 21 X-ray selected AGN from the SUBWAYS sample, detecting radio emission in all sources and finding that 80% show evidence of outflows (winds or weak jets). AGN with Ultra-Fast Outflows (UFOs) tend to have larger radio extensions and steeper radio spectra, with their radio emission consistent with wind-driven shock models, suggesting a possible direct connection between UFOs and radio outflows.
AI.motivation: Disentangling the various mechanisms responsible for radio emission in Radio Quiet AGN (star formation, AGN-driven winds, weak jets, coronal activity) is challenging but crucial for quantifying AGN feedback in galaxy evolution. The SUBWAYS sample, with 30% UFO detection rate, provides a unique opportunity to investigate multi-scale outflows across different frequencies.
AI.method: Multi-wavelength radio investigation of 21 X-ray selected AGN (log Lbol = 44.9-46.3 erg/s, z=0.1-0.5) using proprietary JVLA data at 1.5 and 6 GHz, complemented with LoTSS and other public radio surveys from 150-1400 MHz. Radio SEDs were built and combined with spectral indices, luminosities, morphologies, and multi-waveband properties (SFR, X-ray luminosity, Eddington ratio, UFO kinetic luminosity).
AI.result: All 21 sources detected, consistent with RQ AGN. 80% show evidence of outflows (wind or weak jet). UFO hosts exhibit larger radio extensions and steeper radio spectra. The radio emission of the 6 UFO hosts aligns with predictions from wind-driven shock models.
AI.conclusion: There may be a direct physical connection between UFOs (detected in X-rays) and radio outflows, as UFO hosts show radio properties consistent with wind-driven shocks. Alternatively, this may reflect common physical conditions that favor both phenomena.

title: One's loss is (not) another's gain: Isotropic re-emission destabilizes mass transfer from radiative donor stars
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A Letters; 3 figures
summary: Non-conservative mass transfer plays a central role in close-binary evolution, yet its effects on mass-transfer stability are uncertain. One widely adopted prescription, isotropic re-emission, is often assumed to promote stability compared to conservative mass transfer. We investigate the impact of isotropic re-emission on the stability of mass transfer in binaries with radiative envelopes that undergo delayed dynamical instability (DDI). We assess whether simplified criteria used in binary population synthesis codes accurately capture stability boundaries under varying mass-transfer efficiencies. We perform detailed stellar evolution calculations for a set of representative binaries undergoing DDI. Varying the mass-transfer efficiency beta, we track the onset of instability and quantify the corresponding critical mass ratio. We compare our results with predictions from the commonly used zeta-method, which is based on mass-radius exponents. We find that a lower mass-transfer efficiency destabilizes mass transfer in DDI systems, whereas the zeta-method predicts that isotropic re-emission should stabilize it. The discrepancy arises because the zeta-method fails to capture the full evolution of the orbit and mass ratio during pre-instability mass transfer. In some cases, the critical mass ratio is underestimated by nearly a factor of two. Our findings show that isotropic re-emission can reduce, rather than enhance, DDI stability, underscoring the limitations of using fixed critical mass ratios and zeta-based criteria. This highlights the need for calibrated prescriptions that capture the time-dependent evolution of mass ratio and orbital separation, with direct implications for modelling X-ray binaries, symbiotic stars, and double white dwarfs, including their transient rates and delay-time distributions.

AI.tldr: Isotropic re-emission destabilizes delayed dynamical instability (DDI) in close binaries with radiative envelopes, contrary to the common belief that it promotes stability. The widely used zeta-method fails to capture this, underestimating critical mass ratios by up to a factor of two.
AI.motivation: To investigate the effect of isotropic re-emission (non-conservative mass transfer) on the stability of mass transfer in binaries undergoing delayed dynamical instability, and to assess whether simplified criteria (zeta-method) used in population synthesis correctly capture stability boundaries.
AI.method: Detailed stellar evolution calculations for representative DDI binaries, varying mass-transfer efficiency β, tracking instability onset and critical mass ratio, and comparing results against predictions from the zeta-method based on mass-radius exponents.
AI.result: Lower mass-transfer efficiency destabilizes DDI, opposite to the zeta-method prediction of stabilization. The zeta-method underestimates critical mass ratios by nearly a factor of two, as it fails to capture full orbital and mass-ratio evolution during pre-instability mass transfer.
AI.conclusion: Isotropic re-emission can reduce DDI stability. Fixed critical mass ratios and zeta-based criteria are insufficient; calibrated, time-dependent prescriptions for mass ratio and orbital separation are needed, impacting modeling of X-ray binaries, symbiotic stars, and double white dwarfs.

title: SFUMATO#: a GPU accelerated code for Self-Gravitational Radiation Hydrodynamics Simulation with Adaptive Mesh Refinement
comment: 23 pages, 23 figures, accepted for publication in PASJ
summary: We present a new implementation of the SFUMATO code, called SFUMATO#, for solving self-gravitational radiation hydrodynamics problems using adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) with the CUDA/HIP programming frameworks. The code incorporates a multigrid solver for self-gravity, radiation transfer with M1 closure and reduced speed of light approximation, non-equilibrium chemistry, thermal evolution, and sink particle schemes. We develop new non-equilibrium chemistry and thermal solvers based on a linearized implicit method, whose accuracy is validated through a series of test problems by comparison with solutions obtained using the Newton-Raphson method. By incorporating the heat capacity of dust grains, the dust temperature can be evolved without iterative energy-balance calculations. From the perspective of computational cost, we demonstrate that adopting an increased pseudo dust heat capacity accelerates the chemistry solver while preserving accuracy, even when the value is increased by up to three orders of magnitude relative to the realistic value. In addition, we perform a suite of test problems to confirm the validity of the other components of our implementation. The code supports multi-GPU execution via MPI-based parallelization. We measure the strong-scaling performance of the hydrodynamics and self-gravity solvers on both uniform and AMR grids, as well as the overall code performance using a giant molecular cloud simulation. We find that the computational cost of the self-gravity solver increases with the number of MPI processes, indicating that efficient parallel performance is achieved only when the number of devices is chosen such that the cost of the self-gravity solver remains comparable to that of the other components.

AI.tldr: SFUMATO# is a GPU-accelerated AMR code for self-gravitational radiation hydrodynamics, featuring a linearized implicit chemistry/thermal solver, multi-GPU MPI parallelization, and a novel dust heat capacity acceleration technique. Strong scaling is limited by the self-gravity solver's communication cost.
AI.motivation: To develop a high-performance, GPU-accelerated AMR code (SFUMATO#) for self-gravitational radiation hydrodynamics that efficiently handles non-equilibrium chemistry, thermal evolution, and multi-GPU parallelization, while addressing computational bottlenecks in the chemistry solver via an increased pseudo dust heat capacity technique.
AI.method: The code uses CUDA/HIP for GPU acceleration, a multigrid solver for self-gravity, M1-closure radiation transfer with reduced speed of light approximation, and a linearized implicit method for non-equilibrium chemistry and thermal evolution. Dust temperature is evolved without iterative energy-balance by incorporating dust grain heat capacity. Multi-GPU support uses MPI-based parallelization.
AI.result: The linearized implicit chemistry solver matches Newton-Raphson accuracy. Increasing pseudo dust heat capacity by up to three orders of magnitude accelerates the solver without significant accuracy loss. Strong-scaling tests show the self-gravity solver's communication cost becomes a bottleneck as MPI processes increase, limiting efficient parallel performance.
AI.conclusion: SFUMATO# is a validated, GPU-accelerated AMR code for radiation hydrodynamics. Efficient multi-GPU performance requires balancing the self-gravity solver cost with other components; the dust heat capacity acceleration is a practical technique to reduce chemistry solver runtime.

title: VLTI-GRAVITY measurements of cool evolved stars: II. Pulsation properties and mass-loss process of the Mira star R Car and the red supergiant VX Sgr
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: The mass-loss process of red supergiant (RSG) and asymptotic giant branch (AGB) stars and its relation to variability is poorly constrained. We study two evolved stars, the Mira-type AGB star R Car and the extreme RSG VX Sgr. Our sample comprises 54 VLTI-GRAVITY snapshots taken over 7 years, being the largest VLTI time-series dataset to date. We determine the angular diameter as a function of time. The radii of the photosphere ($R_{\star}$) and atomic atmospheric layers are variable and relate to the light curve with phase shifts, showing a maximum radius near visual brightness minima. The more extended CO layers show longer, irregular periods and maximum extensions of $\sim 1.3-1.7 \: R_{\star}$ for R Car, and of $\sim 1.5-2.2 \: R_{\star}$ for VX Sgr. Comparison with CO5BOLD simulations revealed a similar behavior. Furthermore, during 2020-2021, VX Sgr exhibited an extreme mass-loss event similar to that of Betelgeuse, preceded by two strong shocks and culminating with the extreme expansion of H$_2$O and CO layers, both up to $\sim 2.2 \: R_{\star}$. During this event, we detected Brackett $γ$ and Balmer emission lines, both of which are signatures of a shock propagating through the atmosphere. The Mira R Car showed a photospheric radius $R_{\star} = 280 \pm 25 \: \rm R_\odot$, with a fundamental mode (FM) pulsation amplitude $\sim13 \%$ of $R_{\star} $. During its active cycle, the RSG VX Sgr showed $R_{\star} = 1556 \pm 110 \: \rm R_\odot$ with FM amplitude $ \sim13 \%$ of $R_{\star} $, the same as R Car. During its quiescent cycle, it showed $R_{\star}= 1456 \pm 108 \: \rm R_\odot$ and low-amplitude pulsations near the first overtone, only $\sim4 \%$ of $R_{\star} $. This supports a steady mass loss for Miras related to stable, large-amplitude FM pulsation, whereas the mass-loss process for RSGs may be dominated by extreme events connected to changes in the pulsation mode.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes 7 years of VLTI-GRAVITY data for the Mira-type AGB star R Car and the extreme RSG VX Sgr, tracking angular diameters over time. It finds that photospheric and atmospheric radii vary with phase shifts relative to light curves, with CO layers extending to ~1.3-2.2 R★. VX Sgr experienced an extreme mass-loss event (2020-2021) resembling Betelgeuse's, linked to shocks and pulsation mode changes. R Car exhibits steady FM pulsation (~13% amplitude) supporting steady mass loss, while VX Sgr's mass loss appears dominated by extreme events tied to pulsation mode shifts.
AI.motivation: To constrain the poorly understood mass-loss process of RSG and AGB stars and its relation to variability using long-baseline time-series interferometric observations.
AI.method: Analysis of 54 VLTI-GRAVITY snapshots over 7 years (largest VLTI time-series dataset to date) to determine angular diameters as functions of time, combined with CO5BOLD simulations and spectroscopic diagnostics (Brγ, Balmer lines).
AI.result: Maximum radii of CO layers: R Car ~1.3-1.7 R★, VX Sgr ~1.5-2.2 R★. VX Sgr showed extreme mass-loss event (2020-2021) with shocks and expansion of H2O/CO layers. FM pulsation amplitude ~13% for both stars; VX Sgr quiescent cycle showed ~4% first-overtone pulsation.
AI.conclusion: Steady mass loss in Miras is related to stable, large-amplitude FM pulsation, while RSG mass loss may be dominated by extreme events connected to changes in pulsation mode.

title: Signatures of Very Massive Stars in the Epoch of Reionization
comment: Submitted to A&A Letters, 4 pages and 2 Figures (+Appendix)
summary: We present ultra-deep ($\simeq 20-30$ hours), rest-frame UV spectroscopy with NIRSpec/JWST of two UV-bright galaxies at $z\sim 8.7$, CEERS-1019 and CEERS-1025 ($Z_{\rm neb} \simeq 0.1 Z_{\odot}$). The spectra reveal exceptionally strong P-Cygni profiles in wind lines (NV $λ$1240 and CIV $λ$1550) and significant broad and strong HeII $λ$1640 emission ($\rm EW\simeq 2-4$ A). We compare the observations with synthetic stellar population models at $Z_{\star} \simeq 0.1 Z_{\odot}$, both including and excluding very massive stars (VMS). Models including VMS provide a markedly improved fit to the data relative to non-VMS models ($Δ$AIC and $Δ$BIC $> 70$), which fail to reproduce the observed strengths of the wind features. A comparison with empirical spectra of VMS-dominated systems in the local Universe further supports this interpretation. The best-fit VMS models imply extremely young ages of the stellar populations ($\simeq 1.5-2.0$Myr) and high ionizing photon production efficiencies ($\log ξ_{\rm ion} [\rm Hz erg^{-1}] \gtrsim 25.8$), exceeding those inferred from non-VMS models by $\sim 0.1-0.2$ dex. These results provide evidence for an overabundance of VMS at high-$z$ with an IMF extending well beyond $100 M_{\odot}$, and highlight their potential role in shaping the rest-frame UV spectra, chemical enrichment, and ionizing output of galaxies in the early Universe.

AI.tldr: JWST/NIRSpec ultra-deep spectroscopy of two z~8.7 galaxies reveals strong P-Cygni profiles and HeII emission that can only be explained by stellar population models including very massive stars (VMS), implying top-heavy IMFs with stars >100 M⊙ and high ionizing photon production efficiencies in the early Universe.
AI.motivation: To investigate the presence and role of very massive stars (VMS) in high-redshift galaxies, as VMS may be more abundant in the early Universe and could significantly impact rest-frame UV spectra, chemical enrichment, and ionizing output.
AI.method: Ultra-deep (~20-30 hours) rest-frame UV spectroscopy with NIRSpec/JWST of two z~8.7 galaxies (CEERS-1019 and CEERS-1025); comparison with synthetic stellar population models (Z⋆~0.1 Z⊙) with and without VMS; statistical model selection via ΔAIC and ΔBIC; comparison with empirical VMS-dominated spectra in the local Universe.
AI.result: VMS-inclusive models provide a markedly superior fit (ΔAIC and ΔBIC > 70) to observed wind lines (NV λ1240, CIV λ1550) and broad HeII λ1640 emission (EW~2-4 Å). Best-fit models imply extremely young ages (~1.5-2.0 Myr) and high ionizing photon production efficiencies (log ξion ≳ 25.8 Hz erg⁻¹), ~0.1-0.2 dex higher than non-VMS models.
AI.conclusion: These results provide evidence for an overabundance of very massive stars at high redshift with an IMF extending well beyond 100 M⊙, highlighting their potential role in shaping early galaxy spectra, chemical enrichment, and ionizing output.

title: Tracking Magnetic Topological Change in a Time-Dependent Coronal Model
comment: 14 pages, 5 figures
summary: We apply the slip-back mapping method of Titov et al. 2009 and Lionello et al. 2020 to a thermodynamic MHD simulation to track topological changes in the magnetic field at a range of temporal cadences. The method constitutes the logical successor to a simple open-field map for a steady-state model, as it tracks changes in the open and closed fields for a time-dependent model by tracking individual magnetic elements as they advect across the map, rather than simply tracing field line connectivity from each cell. Through careful categorization of the slip-back mapping values and analysis of the flux changes, we not only effectively track the open flux but can recover the flux processed through interchange reconnection as well. The field lines involved in these processes are shown to follow lines of high squashing factor, as proposed by interchange reconnection-driven slow solar wind theory. The time-dependent model, which is scaled to solar minimum-like activity, projects that a median value of 3.5% of the total open flux in any given 24-hour interval has been processed through interchange reconnection. This corresponds to a relatively high proportion of the total open flux changes over time in the heliosphere. Our results show that not only is this method a useful tool for accurately tracking topological change in time-dependent simulations, but that its inherent complexity can be visually reduced into an intuitive 2D plot that simply and effectively communicates temporal changes.

AI.tldr: The authors apply a slip-back mapping method to a thermodynamic MHD simulation to track magnetic field topological changes over time. By categorizing mapping values and analyzing flux changes, they track open flux and recover flux from interchange reconnection. Results show ~3.5% of total open flux is processed through interchange reconnection per 24 hours during solar minimum-like conditions, demonstrating the method's utility for time-dependent simulations.
AI.motivation: To develop a method that tracks topological changes in magnetic fields for time-dependent MHD simulations, going beyond the limitations of simple steady-state open-field maps, and to quantify the role of interchange reconnection in processing open flux.
AI.method: The slip-back mapping method (Titov et al. 2009; Lionello et al. 2020) is applied to a thermodynamic MHD simulation. Magnetic elements are tracked as they advect across the map, with careful categorization of slip-back mapping values and analysis of flux changes to distinguish open flux changes from interchange reconnection-processed flux.
AI.result: During solar minimum-like conditions, a median of 3.5% of total open flux per 24-hour interval is processed through interchange reconnection, representing a high proportion of open flux changes over time in the heliosphere. Field lines involved follow regions of high squashing factor.
AI.conclusion: The slip-back mapping method is an effective tool for accurately tracking topological changes in time-dependent simulations. Its complexity can be visually reduced into intuitive 2D plots that clearly communicate temporal changes, supporting interchange reconnection-driven slow solar wind theory.

title: SPURS: Bursty Star Formation in an Extremely Luminous Weak Emission Line Galaxy at $z=9.3$
comment: 35 pages, 19 figures, 3 tables. Comments welcome!
summary: JWST has revealed a population of super-luminous early galaxies with a volume density in excess of most expectations. The spectra reveal diverse properties: while some reveal strong emission lines characteristic of galaxies in the midst of strong bursts, others show weak emission lines that could reflect old stellar populations, large escape fractions, or post-burst star formation histories. Through the JWST Cycle 4 large program SPURS, we have obtained ultra-deep (29 hr) rest-frame UV spectroscopy of a z=9.3 super-luminous ($M_{\rm UV}=-21.66$) galaxy with large assembled stellar mass (1.6$\times$10$^9$ $M_\odot$) and extremely weak emission lines (H$β$ EW $\approx25$~Å). The strong stellar wind features and rest-optical line ratios suggest the galaxy is already significantly enriched, with a metallicity of 0.4--0.7~Z$_\odot$. The interstellar absorption lines reveal outflows ($v\simeq -161$~km~s$^{-1}$) with a large neutral gas covering fraction, suggesting that the weak emission lines are not due to large escape fractions. The combination of the Balmer break, weak emission lines, and stellar wind features constrains the star formation history, indicating a recent burst of star formation lasting 10--20 Myr followed by a downturn over the last 10~Myr. The observations suggest that $z\gtrsim 9$ weak emission line galaxies such as this source can be explained by stochastic star formation, provided that the downturns in star formation are recent (i.e., <10 Myr prior to observation). The ultra-deep grating spectrum enables the IGM damping wing to be characterized, decoupling the effects of local absorption. The smooth Ly$α$ break indicates that this source, one of the most massive galaxies known at z>9, is likely situated in a small ionized bubble ($0.29_{-0.09}^{+0.11}$~pMpc), as is common at large neutral hydrogen fractions ($\bar{x}_{\rm HI}=0.81_{-0.21}^{+0.14}$).

AI.tldr: JWST's SPURS program observed a super-luminous z=9.3 galaxy with weak emission lines, high metallicity (0.4-0.7 Z☉), massive stellar content (1.6×10⁹ M☉), and outflows. The weak lines are due to a recent burst followed by a star formation downturn, not large escape fractions. The galaxy sits in a small ionized bubble (0.29 pMpc) in a largely neutral IGM (x_HI=0.81).
AI.motivation: JWST has revealed an unexpectedly high density of super-luminous early galaxies, many with diverse spectral properties—some with strong emission lines, others weak. Understanding the origins of weak emission line galaxies at z>9 is critical to interpreting their star formation histories, enrichment, and contributions to reionization.
AI.method: Ultra-deep (29 hr) rest-frame UV spectroscopy from JWST Cycle 4 SPURS program targeting a z=9.3 super-luminous galaxy. Analysis of stellar wind features, rest-optical line ratios, Balmer break, interstellar absorption lines, and Lyα damping wing profile to constrain metallicity, outflows, covering fraction, star formation history, and IGM neutral fraction.
AI.result: The galaxy has metallicity 0.4-0.7 Z☉, outflow velocity ~ -161 km/s with large neutral gas covering fraction (ruling out large escape fractions as cause of weak lines). Star formation history shows a recent burst (10-20 Myr) followed by a downturn in the last 10 Myr. The Lyα break indicates a small ionized bubble (0.29 pMpc) in a largely neutral IGM (x_HI=0.81).
AI.conclusion: Weak emission line galaxies at z≳9 can be explained by stochastic star formation with recent downturns (<10 Myr prior). These sources are likely embedded in small ionized bubbles as expected at high neutral hydrogen fractions. The ultra-deep spectroscopy enables decoupling of local absorption from IGM damping wing effects.

title: Modeling of the magnetic stellar wind braking of the ssrAp 33 Lib (HD137949)
summary: Using the ssrAp star 33 Lib (HD137949) as an example, we show that magnetic braking caused by the interaction of a strong magnetic field with a stellar wind can play a key role in slowing the rotation of ssrAp stars. Numerical modeling of stellar rotation spin-down in the MESA package, taking into account the evolution of magnetic fields and the stellar wind, shows that stars with rotation periods of up to 80 years and longer can form. Moreover, braking by a magnetized wind makes it possible to estimate the mass-loss rate for stars of moderate mass ($1.25 M_\odot < M < 2 M_\odot$), which is difficult to do by other methods. We introduce the dimensionless parameter $Ξ$, which reflects the spin-down time and the stellar lifetime. Thus, when $Ξ\gg 1$, braking is important, whereas when $Ξ\ll 1$, it is negligible.

AI.tldr: Magnetic braking from stellar wind interaction can slow rotation of ssrAp stars to periods up to 80+ years; the parameter Ξ (spin-down time vs. stellar lifetime) determines braking significance, and this method enables mass-loss rate estimation for moderate-mass stars (1.25–2 M☉).
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that magnetic braking via magnetized stellar wind is a key mechanism for spinning down ssrAp stars, and to provide a way to estimate mass-loss rates for moderate-mass stars where other methods are difficult.
AI.method: Numerical modeling with the MESA stellar evolution code, incorporating magnetic field evolution and stellar wind physics, using the star 33 Lib (HD137949) as a case study. Introduced the dimensionless parameter Ξ = spin-down time / stellar lifetime to quantify braking importance.
AI.result: Magnetic braking can produce rotation periods up to 80 years or longer in ssrAp stars. The parameter Ξ effectively distinguishes regimes where braking is significant (Ξ ≫ 1) versus negligible (Ξ ≪ 1).
AI.conclusion: Magnetized wind braking is a dominant mechanism for the slow rotation of ssrAp stars and offers a novel diagnostic for mass-loss rates in intermediate-mass stars.

title: An Old, Low-mass, Metal-poor Hypervelocity Star Candidate Consistent with a Galactic Center Origin
comment: 10 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables, Accepted for publication in ApJL
summary: We report the discovery of DESI-HVS1, a hypervelocity star (HVS) candidate identified from DESI DR1 spectroscopy and Gaia DR3 astrometry. DESI-HVS1 is an old, low-mass, metal-poor F-type star with a mass of $0.8\,M_\odot$, an age of $\sim14.1$~Gyr, and $\mathrm{[Fe/H]}=-1.6$. It is located at a heliocentric distance of $3.77^{+0.39}_{-0.36}$~kpc and has a Galactocentric total velocity of $523^{+46}_{-47}\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$, marginally exceeding the local escape speed, corresponding to an unbound probability of $P_{\rm ub} \sim 50\%$. Backward orbit integrations show that DESI-HVS1 had a closest approach to the Galactic Centre (GC) of $0.40^{+0.23}_{-0.11}\,\mathrm{kpc}$, with a velocity of $682^{+22}_{-35}\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}}$ and a flight time of $12.89^{+0.92}_{-0.74}\,\mathrm{Myr}$. The reconstructed orbit exhibits a clear perigalactic turning point and only a single crossing of the Galactic midplane ($P_{\rm cross} > 0.95$). These properties suggest that DESI-HVS1 is most naturally explained by the Hills mechanism, although alternative scenarios cannot be entirely ruled out. Its discovery provides the first strong evidence for an old, low-mass HVS candidate consistent with a GC origin, indicating that the apparent dominance of young, massive GC-origin HVSs is likely a consequence of observational selection effects.

AI.tldr: DESI-HVS1 is a newly discovered hypervelocity star (HVS) from DESI DR1 and Gaia DR3. It is an old (14.1 Gyr), low-mass (0.8 M☉), metal-poor ([Fe/H]=-1.6) F-type star with a total velocity of ~523 km/s, marginally exceeding the Galactic escape speed. Orbit integrations suggest a Galactic Center origin via the Hills mechanism, with a closest approach of 0.40 kpc and flight time of ~12.9 Myr. This is the first strong evidence for an old, low-mass HVS from the GC, implying that the observed dominance of young, massive HVSs is due to observational selection effects.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize hypervelocity stars (HVSs) ejected from the Galactic Center, and to investigate whether old, low-mass HVSs exist, challenging the observational bias that previously favored young, massive HVSs.
AI.method: DESI-HVS1 was identified using DESI DR1 spectroscopy and Gaia DR3 astrometry. Its physical properties (mass, age, metallicity) were derived spectroscopically. Kinematic analysis used Gaia astrometry to compute its Galactocentric velocity, and backward orbit integrations were performed to reconstruct its trajectory, determine its perigalactic passage, midplane crossing, and assess the likelihood of a Galactic Center origin via the Hills mechanism.
AI.result: DESI-HVS1 is an old (14.1 Gyr), low-mass (0.8 M☉), metal-poor ([Fe/H]=-1.6) F-type star at 3.77 kpc distance. Its Galactocentric total velocity is 523 km/s, marginally exceeding the local escape speed, with an unbound probability of ~50%. Orbit reconstructions show a closest GC approach of 0.40 kpc at 682 km/s, a flight time of ~12.9 Myr, and a single Galactic midplane crossing (P_cross > 0.95), consistent with the Hills mechanism.
AI.conclusion: DESI-HVS1 provides the first strong evidence for an old, low-mass HVS candidate consistent with a GC origin, suggesting that the predominance of young, massive HVSs in previous samples is an observational selection effect, and that the Hills mechanism can eject stars of various ages and masses from the Galactic Center.

title: Correlation between active regions' spectra at high radio frequencies and solar flare occurrences
comment: 12 pages, 5 figures
summary: High radio frequencies observations with the Italian network of large single-dish radio telescopes resulted in ~450 solar images between 2018 and 2023 in K-band frequency range (18-26 GHz). Solar radio mapping at these frequencies allows the probing of the Active Regions (ARs) chromospheric magnetic field close to the Transition Region, where strong flares and coronal mass ejection events occur. Enhanced magnetic fields up to 1500-2000 G determine anomalous spectra in the ARs brightness compared to pure free-free emission, due to the addition of a steeper gyro-resonance component also associated with circular polarisation up to ~40%. When a significant AR spectral flattening is detected, the probability of a strong flare occurrence within ~30 hours is high (~89% in terms of statistical precision). Despite an approximate weekly cadence of our observations, only ~12% of strong flares are missed/unpredicted within this time interval. Through a correlation analysis, we assess the trade-off on the sensitivity and the robustness of this physics-based flare forecast method.

AI.tldr: Analysis of ~450 solar K-band (18-26 GHz) radio images (2018-2023) from Italian radio telescopes reveals that enhanced chromospheric magnetic fields (1500-2000 G) in Active Regions produce anomalous brightness spectra with gyro-resonance components and circular polarization up to ~40%. When spectral flattening is detected, there is ~89% statistical precision for predicting strong flares within ~30 hours, with only ~12% of strong flares missed despite weekly observation cadence.
AI.motivation: To probe Active Regions' chromospheric magnetic fields near the Transition Region using high-frequency radio observations, and to develop a physics-based method for forecasting strong solar flares and coronal mass ejections.
AI.method: Analysis of ~450 solar images from the Italian network of large single-dish radio telescopes in K-band (18-26 GHz) over 2018-2023, examining brightness spectra, gyro-resonance components, circular polarization, and correlation analysis for flare forecasting.
AI.result: Enhanced magnetic fields (1500-2000 G) cause anomalous spectra with gyro-resonance component and ~40% circular polarization. Spectral flattening predicts strong flares within ~30 hours with ~89% statistical precision; only ~12% of strong flares missed despite weekly cadence.
AI.conclusion: K-band radio observations provide a robust physics-based flare forecasting method with high precision and low miss rate, enabling effective prediction of strong flares even with limited observational cadence.

title: Massive star formation at the Galactic crossroads: Insights from G358.69+0.03 in the Galactic center
comment: Accepted for publication in A&A. 14+11 pages, 18 figures
summary: We investigated the high-mass star formation activity in a subregion of the Sagittarius E star-forming complex, centered at (l,b) = (358.69 deg, 0.03 deg), where infrared and radio sources trace a prominent U-shaped structure that has not been identified in previous studies. We used radio continuum data from the Global View on Star Formation (GLOSTAR) survey, which is a wide-band radio (4-8 GHz) survey of the Milky Way that combines data from the Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array and the Effelsberg 100 m telescope. Using BLOBCAT source extraction software, we identified 49 compact radio sources. Based on multiwavelength associations and spectral index estimates, we identified GLOSTAR counterparts to 27 previously confirmed HII regions, detected radio emission from 3 WISE "radio-quiet" candidates, and report 5 new HII region candidates. The derived physical properties indicate that most are relatively evolved HII regions. We find around 50 cold dust clumps, predominantly toward the south and southeast. Mid-infrared flux-ratio maps ([4.5]/[3.6]) show localized shock enhancements along the arc and adjacent clumps, and 15 clumps exhibit SiO emission with broad components indicative of shocks. Together with CO data, the SiO velocity components delineate a continuous (>100 km/s) velocity bridge that links the far dust-lane inflow to the central molecular zone (CMZ) stream. The largest concentration of clumps and compact HII regions lies at this interface. These combined diagnostics favor a scenario in which bar-driven cloud-cloud collision at the far dust-lane-CMZ interface compressed the gas and triggered the observed high-mass star formation.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes high-mass star formation in a U-shaped subregion of the Sagittarius E complex using GLOSTAR radio data, identifying 49 compact radio sources, 27 confirmed HII regions, 3 WISE "radio-quiet" candidates, and 5 new HII region candidates. Evidence of shocks (SiO emission, mid-IR flux ratios) and a >100 km/s velocity bridge supports bar-driven cloud-cloud collision at the far dust-lane-CMZ interface as the triggering mechanism.
AI.motivation: To investigate high-mass star formation activity in a previously unidentified U-shaped structure within the Sagittarius E star-forming complex, using multiwavelength data to understand the triggering mechanisms.
AI.method: GLOSTAR radio continuum survey (4-8 GHz) combining VLA and Effelsberg 100 m data; BLOBCAT source extraction; multiwavelength associations; spectral index estimates; mid-infrared flux-ratio mapping; CO and SiO line analysis.
AI.result: Identified 49 compact radio sources, 27 confirmed HII regions, 3 WISE "radio-quiet" candidates, and 5 new HII region candidates. Found ~50 cold dust clumps, shock-enhanced mid-IR ratios along the arc, and 15 clumps with broad SiO emission. A continuous >100 km/s velocity bridge links the far dust-lane inflow to the CMZ stream.
AI.conclusion: Bar-driven cloud-cloud collision at the far dust-lane-CMZ interface compressed gas and triggered the observed high-mass star formation.
